ground_truth.title: How meditation rewires brains and improves physical health | Opinion
ground_truth.author: Manoj Jain and Jagpreet Chhatwal
ground_truth.main_content: Our brains hum with electricity. Tiny pulses fire when we are awake, when we concentrate and when we sleep. But what happens when we do something deceptively simple — when we sit in stillness and watch our breath or our thoughts? When these pulses are measured on an electroencephalogram, or EEG — the brain's equivalent of an EKG for the heart — we see something remarkable. Meditation is not a passive act of rest. It produces measurable changes in brain activity, revealing that stillness itself can alter the mind. This has led us to wonder whether the outward stillness and inward calm of meditation can change not only our bodies, but also the world we inhabit. The answer appears to be yes. Meditation is an ancient practice that cuts across faith traditions — from Buddhist monks and Jain ascetics to Sufi mystics and Christian contemplatives. What was once passed down through lineage and scripture is now being examined through the lens of modern science. Researchers are beginning to explore how meditation shapes brain function, influences physiology and may even affect social and collective wellbeing. The timing of this question matters. December 21 has been designated World Meditation Day by the United Nations. The scientific basis of meditation, while still evolving, dates back to the 1960s. Researchers in Boston placed EEG electrodes on Tibetan monks — athletic meditators who had trained for years — and were surprised to find brainwave patterns unlike those observed in non-meditators. Today, advanced imaging tools such as functional MRI show that meditation reshapes neural circuits, calming the amygdala, the brain's center for fear and fight-or-flight responses. Think of the brain as a muscle that strengthens or weakens depending on how it is used. Meditation is a form of mental exercise. Over time, it does more than change the mind — it changes the body. Studies show reductions in stress, blood pressure and anxiety. Meditation also improves sleep, enhances emotional regulation and deepens self-awareness. In individuals with recurrent depression, meditation has been shown to reduce relapse rates by nearly 50%, matching or exceeding antidepressant medications but without the side effects. Mindfulness, or open-monitoring meditation, involves observing thoughts and sensations without judgment. In deeper states of self-transcendence, even these thoughts fall away, giving rise to a profound sense of stillness and clarity. Each meditation state corresponds to a distinct brainwave pattern when measured with an EEG. Delta waves resemble the deep night of dreamless sleep. Theta is the hazy twilight of dreams and imagination. Alpha reflects a calm afternoon — relaxed yet clear. Beta dominates the busy daylight of thinking and doing. And gamma appears in rare peak moments of insight, focus and integration. Beyond individual health, meditation is increasingly being explored as a tool for collective well-being. The United Nations Human Rights Mindfulness Initiative has promoted World Meditation Day to strengthen diplomacy, ethical leadership and human rights work. In a world marked by war, climate instability and widening inequality, meditation offers a counterbalance — a disciplined practice of inner steadiness amid constant disruption. On this World Meditation Day, pause. Silence your devices. Close your eyes. Take a few deep breaths. Even a few minutes of stillness can shift the mind, calm the body and subtly change how we show up in the world. Sometimes, that is where change begins.

The scientific basis of meditation, while still evolving, dates back to the 1960s.
Think of the brain as a muscle that strengthens or weakens depending on how it is used. Meditation is a form of mental exercise.
Meditation also improves sleep, enhances emotional regulation and deepens self-awareness.
In a world marked by war, climate instability and widening inequality, meditation offers a counterbalance — a disciplined practice of inner steadiness amid constant disruption.
